caption: Figure 1. Schematic of specimens for a) tensile and b) impact tests.
paper_abstract: Herein, the relationships among nitrogen content, inclusion characteristics, microstructure, and mechanical properties of low-density steel are investigated. The austenite grain size is refined with nitrogen addition to the low-density steel, and the ultimate tensile strength, plasticity, fracture toughness, and impact toughness are distinctly enhanced. However, the yield strength decreases due to a decrease in the ferrite content with nitrogen addition. AlN is determined to be the main inclusion in the high-nitrogen low-density steel, and the number percentage of AlN that is less than 2 μm reaches 84%, which promotes the refinement of austenite grains. In addition, aggregated AlN inclusions in the dimples of the steel are observed, which are generated during the smelting process, and the aggregated AlN inclusions tend to cause crack initiation in the low-density steel.
paper_title: Effects of Nitrogen on the Microstructure and Mechanical Properties of Fe–28Mn–10Al–0.8C Low-Density Steel
citation_sentences: The machining dimensions of the tensile and impact steel samples are shown in . Tensile tests were conducted Figure 1at room temperature using a universal material testing machine (WDW-200) at a strain rate of $1 \times 1 0 ^ { - 3 } \mathrm { s ^ { - 1 } }$ and tensile speed of 3 mm min1 . The yield strength (YS), UTS, reduction of area (RA), and elongation (A) were determined from the tensile test, and then the stress–strain curves of the tensile test were obtained. The stress–strain curves were corrected by adding the real elastic deformation to plasticity deformation obtained from the tensile test. The impact toughness was tested using an impact testing machine (JB-300B) to obtain the impact energy (CVN) of the steel samples.

citation_sentences: The mechanical properties of the investigated low-density steels are presented in and , which show that the values Figure 2 Table 2of UTS for the low-nitrogen (LN) and high-nitrogen (HN) steels were 832.5 and 841.5 MPa, respectively. The RA and A values for the HN steel were 13% and 12% higher, respectively, than those of the LN steel. In addition, the impact energy of the HN steel was 130.5 J, which was 49.5 J higher than that of the LN steel. Notably, the YS of the HN steel was significantly lower than that of the LN steel. With the addition of nitrogen, the UTS, plasticity, and impact toughness were enhanced, and the YS was reduced.

Researchers have confirmed that steel with fine grains has excellent strength, plasticity, and toughness.[11–18,28] The results of the tensile tests in the present study were consistent with those from the literatures, as indicated in Table 2 and Figure 2. The results showed that the plasticity of the HN steel was obviously better than that of the LN steel, and the LN steel generally broke earlier than the HN steel during local necking. Hans and Roylance et al.[29,30] reported that the areas under stress–strain curves can represent the rated energy consumed by the fracture metal materials. According to the theory, the energy consumption of HN steel fracture is higher than that of LN steel. shows Figure 10the morphologies of the tensile fractures of the LN and HN steels. The macroscopic necking degree of the HN steel was considerably greater than that of the LN steel. Large dimples were observed in the fractures of the HN steel, which was usually surrounded by small dimples. The dimples in the LN steel were smaller in size and greater in number, which indicated that the local stress concentration of the LN steel was more severe during the deformation process. The HN steel was larger and had a deeper depth and more uniform distribution of dimples, which suggested that the plasticity and toughness were improved.[31] The improved plasticity and fracture toughness in the HN steel was due to the fine grains, and many twins caused the deformation to become more uniform and relieve the stress concentration of the deformed steel matrix. In addition, many grain and twin boundaries prevented crack propagation and enhanced fracture toughness.[32,33] Griffith’s equation describes the effect of grain refinement on toughness, as shown in the following equation.[28]

paper_title: Effects of Nitrogen on the Microstructure and Mechanical Properties of Fe–28Mn–10Al–0.8C Low-Density Steel
citation_sentences: shows the microstructures of the LN and HN steels. It Figure 3can be seen that the mean grain sizes of the LN and HN steels were 20.5 and 12.1 μm, respectively. Finer grains were obtained for HN steel. In addition, ferrites with long parallel stripes were observed in the boundaries of austenite matrix due to the lowprecipitation temperature of ferrite. The ferrite in the LN steel was coarser and larger than that in the HN steel. The area percentage of ferrite in the LN steel was 5.08% as compared with 1.84% in the HN steel. The results indicated that nitrogen addition could promote grain refinement and an increase in the area ratio of austenite in Fe–Mn–Al–C low-density steel. In addition, more fine deformation twins were detected in the austenite grains of the HN steel after forging.

To clarify the effects of nitrogen content on the microstructure of low-density steel, a pseudobinary phase diagram of the Fe– 28Mn–10Al–0.8C–N system was calculated using Thermo-Calc software based on the TCFE8 database (see ). The figure Figure 9shows that the maximum formation temperature and temperature range of the austenite phase increased significantly and that ferrite gradually disappeared with the increase in nitrogen content in the steel. As a stabilizing element for austenite, nitrogen expanded the austenite phase area and promoted the growth of the austenite nucleus, thereby preventing the growth of ferrite. As shown in Figure 3, a large number of annealing twins were observed between the austenite grains, which crossed each other and cut the matrix to refine the grains.[24]

where $\sigma _ { \mathrm { s } }$ is the YS of polycrystalline materials, $\sigma _ { 0 }$ is the lattice friction resistance when a single dislocation is moved, d is the average grain diameter, and $K _ { \gamma }$ represents the influence degree of grain boundaries on strength. The YS of polycrystalline materials increased with a decrease in the grain size. In the present study, the increase in nitrogen content led to grain refinement in the HN steel, which explains the improved fracture toughness, tensile strength, and plasticity of the HN steel. However, the YS of the HN steel was slightly lower than that of the LN steel with values of 647 and 685 MPa, respectively, as shown in Table 2. This may have resulted from the different contents of ferrite in the HN and LN steels. The area percentages of ferrite in the LN and HN steels were 5.08% and 1.84%, respectively (see Figure 3). Chen et al.[5] concluded that the YS of steels can be improved by increasing the ferrite content in austenitebased duplex steels.

citation_sentences: show the morphologies of typical inclusions in LN Figure 4, 5and HN steels. The dominant inclusions in the LN steel were AlN, Mn(Mg)S, and their composite inclusions. The sulfide inclusion was elliptical with a smooth edge, and the AlN inclusion had a polyhedral structure. Sulfide inclusions occasionally precipitated on the surface of AlN to form AlN–Mn(Mg)S complex inclusions. For the HN steel, the main inclusion was AlN with a straight edge and sharp angle. It is worth noting that AlN inclusions could be broken after forging (see Figure 5b), which generally became the crack origin of the steel fracture.[23]

citation_sentences: show the morphologies of typical inclusions in LN Figure 4, 5and HN steels. The dominant inclusions in the LN steel were AlN, Mn(Mg)S, and their composite inclusions. The sulfide inclusion was elliptical with a smooth edge, and the AlN inclusion had a polyhedral structure. Sulfide inclusions occasionally precipitated on the surface of AlN to form AlN–Mn(Mg)S complex inclusions. For the HN steel, the main inclusion was AlN with a straight edge and sharp angle. It is worth noting that AlN inclusions could be broken after forging (see Figure 5b), which generally became the crack origin of the steel fracture.[23]

In general, AlN inclusions are generated during the solidification process with lower Al and N contents and can be formed in molten steel with higher Al and N contents. Alba et al.[20] concluded that the main inclusion was AlN–MnS inclusion in Fe–5Mn–3Al steel, whereas the nitrogen content was less than 10 ppm. With an increase in N content, AlN became the dominant inclusion. The results coincided with those observed in the current work, as shown in Figure 4–7.

citation_sentences: show the statistical results of the numbers and Figure 6, 7sizes of inclusions in the LN and HN steels. The number percentages (NPs) of inclusions less than 4 μm in size in the LN and HN steels were 78% and 95%, respectively. The NP value of inclusions (mainly AIN) less than 2 μm in size in the HN steel reached 84%. The total number of inclusions in the HN steel was significantly higher than that in the LN steel, particularly for inclusions less than 2 μm in size. The numbers, sizes, and area ratios of the inclusions are listed in . The number densities were 133 Table 3and 73 mm2 for the HN and LN steels, respectively. Nitrogen addition could increase the number and area ratio of inclusions while decreasing the average size in low-density steels.

citation_sentences: Fe–28Mn–10Al–0.8C steels was calculated and is presented as . In addition, the liquidus (1587 K) and solidus (1493 K) Figure 8of the investigated steel were calculated using FactSage 8.0. The Al and N contents of the LN and HN steels are also indicated in Figure 8, which can be used to examine the formation of AlN inclusions (i.e., by comparison with the stability diagram). For LN steel, the composition of Al and N fell below the equilibrium line of the smelting temperature, which suggests that AlN was not a stable phase and could not be generated during the smelting process. However, for HN steel with 0.1326 wt% nitrogen, the composition far exceeded the equilibrium line. The results indicate that AlN inclusions can be easily formed and stably exist during the smelting process.

Large dimples were observed in the fractures of the HN steel, which was usually surrounded by small dimples. The dimples in the LN steel were smaller in size and greater in number, which indicated that the local stress concentration of the LN steel was more severe during the deformation process. In addition, the fracture morphology of the HN steel had a crater shape, whereas the fracture section of the LN steel was $4 5 ^ { \circ }$ to the load direction, as shown in Figure 10. The results revealed that the LN steel sample was fractured by shear stress at an angle of $4 5 ^ { \circ }$ before the stress in the loading direction reached its tensile strength. Therefore, the tensile strength of the LN steel was lower than that of HN steel. The results were derived from the many grain and twin boundaries in the steel matrix. The grain and twin boundaries also hindered the dislocation movement in the HN steel, thereby indirectly promoting the dislocation strengthening effect and further enhancing the strength and hardness of the HN steel.

citation_sentences: shows the morphologies of the impact fractures of Figure 11LN and HN steels. In general, fine grains and twins considerably improve the impact toughness of steel.[15,35] In our study, the grain boundaries of metal materials with fine grains were more curved in the HN steel, which could prevent crack propagation and absorb more impact energy during the impact test. Meanwhile, the ferrite precipitated at the grain boundary of austenite matrix contributes to crack initiation and brittle crack propagation.[36,37] The impact fractures of the LN and HN steel samples presented a standard ductile impact fracture, which included the fibrous, radiation, and shear lip zones. The area ratio of the shear lip zone in the HN steel sample was significantly higher than that in the LN steel, indicating that the crack growth rate of the HN steel was slower and the impact toughness was better. Figure 11a shows that the fracture dimples of the LN steel were elongated in the direction of crack propagation, which was greatly affected by the shear force, resulting in accelerated crack propagation. Figure 11b shows that the size and average depth of the fracture dimples of the HN steel were large and uniform. The HN steel matrix exhibited higher deformation and absorbed more energy during the impact test.

caption: Figure 12. Typical inclusions in the dimples of a fracture surface.
paper_abstract: Herein, the relationships among nitrogen content, inclusion characteristics, microstructure, and mechanical properties of low-density steel are investigated. The austenite grain size is refined with nitrogen addition to the low-density steel, and the ultimate tensile strength, plasticity, fracture toughness, and impact toughness are distinctly enhanced. However, the yield strength decreases due to a decrease in the ferrite content with nitrogen addition. AlN is determined to be the main inclusion in the high-nitrogen low-density steel, and the number percentage of AlN that is less than 2 μm reaches 84%, which promotes the refinement of austenite grains. In addition, aggregated AlN inclusions in the dimples of the steel are observed, which are generated during the smelting process, and the aggregated AlN inclusions tend to cause crack initiation in the low-density steel.
paper_title: Effects of Nitrogen on the Microstructure and Mechanical Properties of Fe–28Mn–10Al–0.8C Low-Density Steel
citation_sentences: Finally, the changes in the number and sizes of inclusions derived from nitrogen addition also had obvious effects on the mechanical properties of the HN steel. Different types of inclusions were detected in the dimples on the fracture surfaces of the test samples, as shown in , indicating that these inclu-Figure 12sions became the initial point of fracture. The sulfide inclusions showed good deformation ability and were typically large; however, these sulfide inclusions could easily break away from cracks at the inclusion–matrix interface due to their weak binding force to the steel matrix.[38,39] In comparison, the brittle AlN inclusions had a high melting point and exhibited poor deformation ability. For HN steel, fine and dispersed AlN inclusions could refine the austenite grains and improve their mechanical properties.

However, aggregated AlN inclusions were detected in the HN steel (see Figure 12b), which could deteriorate the steel properties. Wang et al.[22] also observed aggregated AlN inclusions in low-density steel and concluded that aggregated AlN inclusions were generated during the smelting process due to the combined effects of the cavity bridge and viscous resistance.
